title: Cubby Dresser with No-Slide Drawers
summary: A six-drawer dresser whose drawers rest on 1x2 guides between two identical 2x4 face frames — no drawer slide hardware anywhere, a genuinely different build method than any other dresser here.
description:
This dresser's defining trick is skipping drawer slide hardware entirely: two identical 2x4 face frames, front and back, are joined by horizontal 2x4s that the drawers simply glide across on 1x2 guides screwed down on top. **Getting the two face frames genuinely identical is the single most important thing in this build** — the plan is explicit that mismatched frames are what makes drawers run cockeyed, so build them side by side and check them against each other before joining anything else.

Once the frames are joined by the horizontal 2x4s, the 1x2 guides go on top, screwed down loosely with just two screws each so they can be adjusted later if a drawer binds — a deliberate under-fastening, not an oversight. **Build each drawer box 1/4" smaller in width and height than its opening**, leaving a consistent 1/8" gap on all sides once installed; too tight and a plain wood-on-wood slide binds immediately, no hardware forgiving the error the way metal slides would.

Side panels, trim, and the top go on last, and the top gets built separately with pocket holes before final attachment — treat it as its own small subassembly rather than something you fasten piece by piece in place, since keeping it flat and square off to the side is easier than fighting gravity and clamps on top of an already-standing dresser.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Should match the width of three 1x6s.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four face frame verticals at 31", two face frame drawer horizontals at 43-1/2", ten connecting rails at 16-1/2", six top boards at 47-1/2" and two top end boards at 21" from the 2x4. Cut four face frame top/bottom horizontals at 43-1/2" and the mid uprights at 11-1/2" from the 2x2 — note the cut list says four uprights, but three bays per row needs two, and there are two rows in each of the two frames, so cut EIGHT. Cut eight drawer guides at 16-1/2" and four side trim at 16-1/2" from the 1x2. Cut six side panels at 29-1/2" from the 1x6, and two side trim at 16-1/2" from the 1x4. From the 1x10 cut TWELVE drawer sides at 12-1/4" and twelve drawer fronts and backs at 17" — twelve sides, not the six the cut list shows, since six boxes need two apiece; that is 351" of 1x10, which fills four 8 ft boards with nothing to spare, so buy 32 linear feet rather than the 24 declared. Cut six drawer bottoms at 13-1/4" from the 1/4" plywood and six drawer faces at 13-1/4" from the 1x12. Hold the drawer parts back from final size until you have measured a real bay in step 5 — the listed widths do not fit the openings this frame produces. Square every cut and label each piece.

steps.title: Build the two face frames
steps.body: Each frame is two 31" 2x4 verticals with the 43-1/2" horizontals between them: a 2x2 across the top, a 2x2 across the bottom, and the 3-1/2" 2x4 drawer horizontal splitting the frame into two drawer rows. Two 11-1/2" 2x2 mid uprights in each row divide it into three bays — 43-1/2" less two 1-1/2" uprights, over three, is 13-1/2" per bay, which is what the 13-1/4" drawer faces are sized to leave an even 1/8" reveal against. Build both frames side by side on the same flat surface, using pocket holes throughout, and lay one on the other before you join anything: mismatched frames are the single thing that makes these hardware-free drawers run cockeyed, and there is no slide to forgive it. Drill upward-facing pocket holes along the inside of the top horizontals now, for attaching the top much later.

steps.title: Join the frames
steps.body: Stand the two frames parallel and join them with the ten 16-1/2" connecting rails, running front to back — one pair at each end and one pair at each mid upright, so every bay has a rail down each side to carry a guide. That 16-1/2" span plus the 1-1/2" thickness of each frame makes the case 19-1/2" deep, and 43-1/2" plus two 3-1/2" verticals makes it 50-1/2" wide. Sight down the case from one end as you fasten: the two frames have to end up directly in line, not just parallel, or every drawer will run out of square with its own opening.

steps.title: Add the drawer guides
steps.body: Screw the eight 16-1/2" 1x2 guides flat on top of the connecting rails, one down each side of every bay, square to the front face frame — a guide that toes in even slightly will bind a wood-on-wood drawer at the back of its travel. Use only TWO screws per guide and no glue. That is deliberate under-fastening, not an oversight: with no slide hardware to adjust, moving a guide 1/16" is the only correction available once a drawer binds, and you want to be able to back the screws out and shift it.

steps.title: Build the drawer boxes
steps.body: Measure a real bay before cutting anything to final size. Built to plan the openings are 13-1/2" wide by 11-1/2" tall, and the rule is to build each box 1/4" under in both directions — 13-1/4" by 11-1/4" — leaving a consistent 1/8" gap all round. Be warned that the cut list disagrees with its own frame here: the drawer fronts and backs are drawn at 17" and the bottoms at 17-3/4", both several inches wider than the 13-1/2" bay they must enter, and the box does not close against that bottom in either orientation. Cut the fronts and backs to your measured opening less 1/4", run the 12-1/4" sides front to back inside the 16-1/2" case depth, and cut each bottom to the box you actually built rather than to the listed size. Assemble with glue and 1-1/4" brads, checking each box for square — too tight and a bare wood drawer binds on the first pull, with no metal slide to hide the error.

steps.title: Attach the side panels
steps.body: Slide every drawer in and out through its full travel before the sides go on — once the panels are fastened you lose the access that lets you reach in and shift a guide. When you are satisfied, attach three 29-1/2" 1x6 side panels to each end of the case, standing them on edge next to each other; three 5-1/2" boards make 16-1/2", which is exactly the depth of the connecting rails they land on. Fasten through pocket holes into the frame verticals and rails so nothing shows on the outside face.

steps.title: Trim the side panels
steps.body: Trim each side with the 16-1/2" 1x2 and 1x4 pieces so the ends read like the front — the 1x4 gives a wider band and the 1x2 a narrower one, and both are cut to the 16-1/2" side depth. Hold them to the same lines as the front face frame's horizontals so the trim wraps the corner instead of stopping at a random height. Glue and pin with 1-1/4" brads, then set the nails and fill.

steps.title: Build and attach the top
steps.body: Build the top flat on the bench as its own slab: six 47-1/2" 2x4 laid side by side make 21", with a 21" end board capping each end, so the finished top is 50-1/2" wide by 21" deep. Join it with pocket holes and glue while it is off the dresser — keeping a slab this size flat is far easier on a bench than fighting clamps and gravity on top of a standing case. Set it on the dresser and pull it down through the upward-facing pocket holes you drilled in the frames: 50-1/2" matches the case width exactly, so it lands flush at both ends and stands 3/4" proud front and back over the 19-1/2" case. Then attach the six drawer faces over the closed boxes on 1/8" spacers, fit the pulls, sand and finish.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Six-drawer cubby dresser with no drawer slide hardware
